Name: JARS-Observational
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 3: Reporting Standards for Studies Using No Experimental Manipulation
Description: The JARS-Quant observational table adapts psychology-specific reporting expectations for nonexperimental studies, including single-group designs, natural-group comparisons, and observational or correlational studies.
Aim: To improve the reporting of observational and other nonexperimental psychology studies by clarifying expectations for design description, participant selection, variables, comparability of assessment, analysis, and limitations.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and behavioral-science studies that do not involve an experimental manipulation, including single-group analyses and comparisons across naturally occurring groups.
Scope.Focus: Study design, data type, participant selection, data sources, variable definition and measurement, comparability across groups, confounder handling, and study limitations.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for manipulated experiments, clinical trials, or research syntheses. This module supplements the JARS-Quant master table for nonexperimental designs.

primary.description: Appelbaum M, Cooper H, Kline RB, Mayo-Wilson E, Nezu AM, Rao SM. Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology: The APA Publications and Communications Board Task Force Report. American Psychologist. 2018. PMID: 29345484.

items.item_name: State study design and data use
items.item_question: Does the manuscript clearly describe the nonexperimental design and the type of data used?
items.item_question_background: Readers need to know whether the study is observational, correlational, natural-history, archival, or another nonexperimental form before they can interpret its claims.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract the named study design and the type or source of data used.

source_page_refs.quote: Study design ... Describe the design of the study. Data use ... State the type of data used.

items.item_name: Describe participant selection and data sources
items.item_question: Does the manuscript explain how participants or units were selected for each group and identify any data sources, codes, or record-linkage procedures used?
items.item_question_background: Selection and data-source transparency are essential for evaluating sampling validity, comparability, and reproducibility in nonexperimental work.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract participant selection methods, group counts, matching criteria, and any record-linkage codes, algorithms, or archival sources used.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe the method(s) of selecting participants ... Identify data sources used ... include codes or algorithms to select participants or link records.

items.item_name: Define variables and comparability of assessment
items.item_question: Does the manuscript define exposures, predictors, confounders, and effect modifiers and explain how variables were measured and how assessment comparability was handled across groups?
items.item_question_background: Without clear definitions and comparable assessment procedures, observed group differences may reflect measurement differences rather than substantive effects.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract definitions and measurement of exposures, predictors, confounders, and effect modifiers, plus any explanation of cross-group assessment comparability.

source_page_refs.quote: Define all variables clearly ... Describe comparability of assessment across groups.

items.item_name: Explain analytic treatment of confounding and effect modification
items.item_question: Does the manuscript describe how predictors, confounders, and effect modifiers were incorporated into the analysis?
items.item_question_background: Nonexperimental designs depend heavily on transparent analytic handling of confounders and effect modifiers to make associations interpretable.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract how predictors, confounders, and effect modifiers entered the analytic model or comparison strategy.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe how predictors, confounders, and effect modifiers were included in the analysis.

items.item_question: Does the manuscript discuss limitations such as misclassification, unmeasured confounding, or changing eligibility criteria over time?
items.item_question_background: JARS-Observational expects authors to surface the major inferential weaknesses that commonly affect nonexperimental studies.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any discussion of misclassification, unmeasured confounding, changing eligibility criteria, or other design-specific limitations.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe potential limitations of the study ... misclassification, unmeasured confounding, and changing eligibility criteria over time.

Aliases: Reporting Standards for Studies Using No Experimental Manipulation
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology observational study reports participant selection, record-linkage rules, confounders, and comparability of assessment using the JARS observational module.
A natural-group comparison paper explicitly cites JARS Table 3 and discusses misclassification and unmeasured confounding.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized experiment with treatment delivery details should map to JARS-Experimental instead.
A research synthesis with no primary participants is not a JARS-Observational match.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts with no experimental manipulation, especially observational, correlational, archival, or natural-group comparison designs.
